paragraphs.body: Salt Circle

The man wearing his ass like it’s a ball cap goes by Roy, but Sissy knows him as the chode-having park operator. Chode-having asshat park operator Roy is only here because November rent is late, which Sissy knows because she had to steal the propane tank from her neighbor’s trailer because Sissy is dirt poor. Basically, she’s up shit’s creek without a paddle.

annotations.text: his ass like it’s a ball cap
annotations.comment: Really could not picture what this means. But after I got into the first paragraph, I was intrigued. I didn’t follow that Sissy knew the rent was late because she had to steal the tank? I think you mean something more along the lines of those two events going together in someway?

paragraphs.body: “I know ya’ll’ve had it rough. What with yer momma takin’ off and leavin’ ya with Junie, but Sissy-,” Roy starts, spitting out a black gob of chew, “I’m gonna haffa ask ya to get goin’. Nuthin’ I can do ‘bout it if ya ain’t payin’. On the bright side, there’s a motel over yonder, ya’ll could-”

annotations.comment: I could picture this guy (and honestly, he seems nicer than the earlier description of him implied). I think you can cut back on the dialect a bit. Just a y’all and a gonna here and there. You don’t need to try to render every word as he would say it.

paragraphs.body: “Don’t piss on my leg and say it’s rainin’, Roy.” Sissy crosses her arms and leans against the trailer door. “How much time I got?” Roy glances at Junebug, playing by her lonesome in the patchy grass, before meeting Sissy’s eyes again.
“End of the week. And I need it all, Sissy.”
“Ima getcha every nickel and dime.” Roy nods, patting the younger girl’s head and walking off.

Junebug, crouching down so her knobby knees are pressed against her chest, pours salt from the spigot of the Morton tin slowly and methodically onto the dirt path in a wobbly circle. Inside, a slug, in all its slimy, mucus-secreting glory, stares up at her through drooping optical tentacles. His little alien face begs to be killed without all the pomp and circumstance. Junebug’s tongue juts from her mouth, curling up toward her lip in determination as she brings the spout back to the initial pile of salt, aptly locking the slug behind a set of salty prison bars. Her coppery blonde pigtails hang before her moss-green eyes as she leans in close, examining her captive.

annotations.comment: I was a little surprised that a child would know how to do this.

paragraphs.body: “Don’t go wastin’ all the salt, Junebug,” Sissy chews out between lip smacks before hucking a wad of minty green gum into the small patch of grass some may call a yard. Sissy wouldn’t define it so classy-like. Junebug hops up and brushes her hands together, slapping away any lingering salt. “You oughta leave the lil’ feller be. He ain’t do nothin’ to no one.”

annotations.comment: I think you’re working a little too hard to get it across the Sissy is poor and uneducated.

paragraphs.body: “He’s ugly lookin’, that’s good ‘nough for me.” Junebug grins, her yellowing gap-teeth making a spectacle of her mouth.
“Get your hiney cleaned up, you gotta go to Annie’s. I’m pickin’ up a shift.” Junebug groans, rolling her eyes and stomping up into the mobile home.

When Sissy says she’s “pickin’ up a shift,” it’s not entirely true. It’s not true at all, actually, because she doesn't have a job. She’s tried, so don’t go off saying she’s a lazy no-good bottom-feeder, but it’s hard to get something decent in a dead-end town where everyone is up in everyone’s business, especially Sissy and her momma’s. So, when Sissy “picks up a shift,” she’s really thievin’. Just little stuff—a watch here, a couple bucks there—but it adds up, in her pocket and in her soul. Annie doesn’t know about the thievin’. Junebug neither. People tend to look down on that, even someone like Annie, who is just as sweet as honey. She used to go to school with Sissy ‘til Mamma got goin’ and Sissy had to drop out. She lives near the trailer park and is good about letting Junebug mess around in her backyard. It’s not a big house, not too pretty neither, but she’s got a real yard and doesn’t have to steal propane. Sissy can’t imagine being able to get a house of her own. She doesn’t even have a high school diploma, let alone any kind of future outside of thievin’ and Junebug.

annotations.comment: This made me laugh since it’s the narrator who’s been telling me about Sissy and Junebug.
This was a nice twist. Not what I expected.
annotations.context_left: ne’s business, especially Sissy and her momma’s. So, when Sissy “picks up a shift,” she’s really thi
annotations.context_right: . Just little stuff—a watch here, a couple bucks there—but it adds up, in her pocket and in her soul

annotations.comment: I don’t know who Annie is. I did wonder what Annie thinks Sissy is doing, as well as how Sissy converts her thefts into cash.
annotations.context_left: Just little stuff—a watch here, a couple bucks there—but it adds up, in her pocket and in her soul.
annotations.context_right: doesn’t know about the thievin’. Junebug neither. People tend to look down on that, even someone li

annotations.comment: I liked this glimpse into the future Sissy can’t quite imagine for herself.
annotations.context_left: e to get a house of

paragraphs.body: When Sissy goes thievin’, she marches down the road until she comes up on a little shithole bar, where all the drunks from last night are still passed out in the booths and stools, and slips her hands into their jean pockets or maybe their jackets. Sometimes the drunk wakes up and throws a fit, but all Sissy has to do is flash a smile and wet her lips with the swipe of her tongue and lean over the bar, so her cleavage starts showing. They tend to ease up. Most of ‘em never even notice something’s missing.

annotations.comment: Yeah, I didn’t buy this.
I mean, people own and work at this bar. What do they think of Sissy’s habit?

paragraphs.body: So, she marches down to the bar, and she reaches into the pockets, and one of the drunks stirs but stays sleeping, so Sissy leaves with a watch to pawn and fifty-three dollars in cash. She thumbs through the cash as she walks up on the pawn shop. The owner is real sweet, and always gives her a little more than it’s worth. The watch goes for a hundred bucks or so, and Sissy starts home. But as she’s heading back to the park and double wide, she sees this familiar little girl walking alongside her momma. They used to live in the park with their daddy, not too far from Sissy and Junebug. The girl’s wearing pink overalls and pigtails with bows, and she’s even got a little stuffed bear stuffed in the crick of her elbow. And her momma’s wearing a nice dress, a conservative blue one that covers up her chest and falls just below her knees. She smiles at her baby, and Sissy can hear them talking.

annotations.text: gives her a little more than it’s worth
annotations.comment: That’s a pawn shop that won’t stay in business long.

paragraphs.body: “Why don’t we live in the park no more, Momma?” the little girl asks.

annotations.comment: This dialogue felt a little heavy handed and on the nose.

paragraphs.body: “Cus Momma got a job at the school, and Daddy cleaned up his act, and we got a little place. A nice place for you to get big in.”
“Will we ever go back?”
“No,” her momma says, stopping and crouching down to be face-to-face with her girl, “We ain’t never goin’ back there. We’re better than that dump. We’re goin’ places.”

annotations.comment: You might consider retrying this as dialogue between the characters. It might teach us a bit more about Sissy and her situation if she’s like, oh, hey, haven’t seen you in a while.

paragraphs.body: Junebug is already back at the trailer when Sissy returns one hundred fifty-three dollars richer. She’s out in the patchy lawn, poking at the slug in the salt circle. She looks up at Sissy, grinning like a possum eating persimmons, and points at her captive.

annotations.comment: How did she get back?

paragraphs.body: “He’s called Slugger. Ain’t he just ugly?” Junebug asks. Sissy chuckles and smiles.
“You look like that lil’ feller,” she teases, ruffling June’s hair. She sticks her tongue out at her older sister and stands, brushing the dirt and salt from her knobby knees.

annotations.comment: Ah, this is the first time I understood that Junebug isn’t Sissy’s child.

paragraphs.body: “Didya pick up a good shift?” Sissy nods, pulling out some crumpled cash from her pocket. “Roy gonna be happier than a dead pig in the sunshine.”
“Hush up, Junie,” Sissy chuckles, glancing down at the slug in his cage, “and you better let that lil’ guy go. He gon’ die in that place.”
Junebug shrugs, “Well he still kickin’, ain’t he?”

“I guess he is,” Sissy sighs, “Now get up in that house and get to bed. You got school in the mornin’.” Sissy opens the door for her sister and watches as she jaunts into the home, though Sissy’s eyes linger on the critter stuck in the salt circle.

annotations.comment: The timing here is a little confusing — did Sissy take all day to rob a couple of people and sell a watch at the pawnshop? Can you help us understand that $153 isn’t enough and that Sissy knows that?

paragraphs.body: As Junebug sleeps soundly in the bed, Sissy gets herself as dolled up as she can and makes the trek back to the shithole bar. It’s livelier once the sun has retired, flashing lights and loud music blasting out of the joint. The familiar sound of cowboy boots banging and stomping on the old wooden panels brings a reminiscent smile to Sissy’s face. She’s taken back to a simpler time—a time when Sissy and Annie would sneak in through the small window in the women’s restroom (always cracked open for a sly indoor smoke) and flirt with the men at the bar so they could get a few strong drinks in before the bartender would threaten to call their mommas.

annotations.comment: I’m a little confused about the age difference between the sisters — Sissy is in her 20’s? But Junebug is a child?

paragraphs.body: “Hey, Sissy,” a sweet as honey voice sounds from the bar. Sissy’s eyes lock onto Annie and her frizzy black hair stuffed into a bun on top of her head. She’s standing behind the bar, a shaker in one hand and an ice scooper in the other. Sissy meets Annie across the bar.
“I ain’t know you was workin’ here, Annie.”
Annie nods proudly, dumping the ice in the shaker and reaching behind her for some liquor, “It pays the bills.”
“Does it really?” Sissy scoffs. Annie nods again, pouring the liquor into the shaker.

annotations.comment: Very funny that Sissy is so dismissive of, like, a real job.

paragraphs.body: “Sure it does.”
“I thought you was goin’ to school?”

“I am. My momma bought me a laptop. I do it online. I’m getting my degree in architecture,” Annie explains, finishing the drink and pouring it into a glass before sliding it across the bar to some old drunk guy with a big gray beard. He looks close to passing out. A good target.
“Well, thanks for watchin’ Junie. I’ll be seein’ ya,” she starts, beginning to make a break for drunk Santa.
“Hold on, Sissy. Where’ve you been working?” Annie questions. Sissy pauses for a moment, really thinking up a good answer.

annotations.comment: This is a conversation that likely would have made more sense when Sissy dropped Junie off.

paragraphs.body: “Here n’ there.” She shrugs, tucking a piece of hair behind her ear.
“Would you want to work here? Billy’s looking for another bartender to cover Friday nights. It would kind of be like old times. You remember when we used to sneak in here, don’t you Sissy?”
“I don’t know nothin’ ‘bout bartendin’ though.”

annotations.comment: I buy it, but boy is it annoying that Sissy doesn’t jump at this job.

paragraphs.body: “That’s alright. I’ll teach you.” Annie smiles, teeth pretty and white and shining like the stars up in the sky.

annotations.comment: Does Annie have the right to hire someone?

paragraphs.body: “Alright. Yeah, that sounds nice,” Sissy says coyly, smiling quickly and nodding simply. She doesn’t stay for a drink, and she pays no mind to the sleeping Santa Claus resting his head on the bar. Instead, she goes back to the double wide. When she arrives, she takes a nice long look at the slug and smiles, crouching down so her knees are to her chest, and drags her finger through the salt circle. “Go on,” she grins, “get on outta here, feller.”

annotations.comment: I like a lot of the story draft, here, Zoe. It’s really a story about finding some small hope, and I am always drawn to that. And I liked all the women in this story — their care for each other was lovely. I think you’ve put your thumb down pretty heavily on the scale in terms of letting us know that Sissy and Junie are poor. In your eagerness to convey that, you’ve overlooked the plot a bit. It’s hard to buy that Sissy routinely robs people at the local bar without any repercussions, and that she would eagerly take a job there despite that history. I also didn’t understand why Annie would suddenly be able to hire Sissy and wouldn’t have mentioned that earlier, when Sissy dropped off Junie. So I think you need to rethink that a bit. Maybe the women converse at the drop off, or maybe Sissy takes Junie somewhere else besides Annie’s? Maybe Sissy isn’t constantly thievin’ (so much so that they have a term for it) and instead tries it for the first time? Maybe she asks Annie for the job? Not sure how you want to se tthat up, but right now, things just sort of easily work out great for Sissy, which is nice for her but doesn’t make for a story with a lot of drive. A couple of other thoughts: you intriguingly note that Sissy feels guilty about stealing, so I wondered if that comes up again at any point (or stops her from thieving, maybe?). And, as mentioned, I’m perplexed by the huge age difference in these sisters. Thanks, Rileigh and happy revising!
